questions.text: State the principle of quantization of electric charge.
questions.expected_answer: The total charge on a body is always an integral multiple of the elementary charge.

questions.text: Why do electric field lines never cross each other?
questions.expected_answer: If two field lines crossed, the electric field would have two directions at the same point, which is impossible.

questions.text: Define electric dipole moment and state its SI unit.
questions.expected_answer: Electric dipole moment is the product of either charge and separation between the charges; its SI unit is coulomb meter.

questions.text: Explain the physical significance of electric flux.
questions.expected_answer: Electric flux represents the total electric field passing normally through a given surface.

questions.text: State Gauss's law in electrostatics.
questions.expected_answer: The total electric flux through a closed surface equals the net charge enclosed divided by the permittivity of free space.

questions.text: What is an equipotential surface?
questions.expected_answer: An equipotential surface is a surface on which electric potential has the same value at every point.

questions.text: Why is electrostatic potential constant throughout a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium?
questions.expected_answer: The electric field inside the conductor is zero, so no work is done in moving charge within it.

questions.text: Define dielectric constant of a medium.
questions.expected_answer: Dielectric constant is the ratio of the permittivity of the medium to the permittivity of free space.

questions.text: Explain the effect of introducing a dielectric slab between plates of a parallel plate capacitor.
questions.expected_answer: The capacitance increases by a factor equal to the dielectric constant when the slab fully fills the space.

questions.text: What is electrostatic shielding?
questions.expected_answer: Electrostatic shielding is protecting a region from external electric fields using a conducting enclosure.

questions.text: Define drift velocity of free electrons in a conductor.
questions.expected_answer: Drift velocity is the average velocity with which free electrons move through a conductor under an applied electric field.

questions.text: How does relaxation time change with increase in temperature?
questions.expected_answer: Relaxation time decreases because higher temperature increases lattice vibrations and electron collisions.

questions.text: State Ohm's law.
questions.expected_answer: Current through a conductor is directly proportional to potential difference across it when physical conditions remain constant.

questions.text: What are ohmic and non-ohmic devices?
questions.expected_answer: Ohmic devices obey Ohm's law with a linear V-I relation, while non-ohmic devices do not.

questions.text: Define electrical resistivity.
questions.expected_answer: Resistivity is the resistance of a conductor of unit length and unit cross-sectional area.

questions.text: State Kirchhoff's junction rule.
questions.expected_answer: The algebraic sum of currents meeting at a junction is zero, based on conservation of charge.

questions.text: What is the principle of a Wheatstone bridge?
questions.expected_answer: In the balanced condition no current flows through the galvanometer and the ratios of resistances in the arms are equal.

questions.text: Kirchhoff's loop rule is based on which conservation principle?
questions.expected_answer: It is based on the law of conservation of energy.

questions.text: Why is a potentiometer preferred over a voltmeter for measuring emf?
questions.expected_answer: At null point a potentiometer draws no current from the cell, so it measures true emf.

questions.text: Define internal resistance of a cell.
questions.expected_answer: Internal resistance is the resistance offered inside a cell by its electrolyte and electrodes to current flow.

questions.text: State the Biot-Savart law.
questions.expected_answer: The magnetic field due to a current element is proportional to current and element length, and inversely proportional to the square of distance.

questions.text: What is the magnetic field at the center of a circular current-carrying loop proportional to?
questions.expected_answer: It is directly proportional to the current and inversely proportional to the radius of the loop.

questions.text: Define Lorentz force.
questions.expected_answer: Lorentz force is the total force on a charged particle moving in electric and magnetic fields.

questions.text: When does a charged particle moving in a magnetic field experience no magnetic force?
questions.expected_answer: It experiences no force when it moves parallel or anti-parallel to the magnetic field.

questions.text: State Ampere's circuital law.
questions.expected_answer: The line integral of magnetic field around a closed path equals permeability of free space times the current enclosed.

questions.text: What is a cyclotron?
questions.expected_answer: A cyclotron accelerates charged particles to high energies using perpendicular electric and magnetic fields.

questions.text: Why is a radial magnetic field used in a moving coil galvanometer?
questions.expected_answer: It keeps the torque proportional to current by keeping the coil plane effectively parallel to the magnetic field.

questions.text: How can a galvanometer be converted into an ammeter?
questions.expected_answer: By connecting a low resistance shunt in parallel with the galvanometer.

questions.text: What is the force between two parallel current-carrying conductors?
questions.expected_answer: Conductors attract when currents are in the same direction and repel when currents are opposite.

questions.text: Define one ampere using parallel conductors.
questions.expected_answer: One ampere is the current that produces a force of 2 x 10^-7 newton per meter between two long parallel conductors one meter apart in vacuum.

questions.text: State Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction.
questions.expected_answer: The induced emf in a circuit is proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux linked with it.

questions.text: What does the negative sign in Faraday's law indicate?
questions.expected_answer: It indicates that induced emf opposes the change in magnetic flux, according to Lenz's law.

questions.text: State Lenz's law.
questions.expected_answer: The induced current is in a direction such that it opposes the change in magnetic flux producing it.

questions.text: How does Lenz's law obey conservation of energy?
questions.expected_answer: Work done against the opposing induced current is converted into electrical energy.

questions.text: What are eddy currents?
questions.expected_answer: Eddy currents are circulating currents induced in bulk conductors exposed to changing magnetic flux.

questions.text: Define self-inductance.
questions.expected_answer: Self-inductance is the property of a coil by which it opposes changes in current through induced emf in itself.

questions.text: What is the SI unit of inductance?
questions.expected_answer: The SI unit of inductance is henry.

questions.text: Define mutual inductance between two coils.
questions.expected_answer: Mutual inductance is the property by which changing current in one coil induces emf in another nearby coil.

questions.text: On what factors does mutual inductance depend?
questions.expected_answer: It depends on geometry, separation, orientation, number of turns, and permeability of the core medium.

questions.text: Explain the principle of an AC generator.
questions.expected_answer: An AC generator works on electromagnetic induction, converting mechanical energy into electrical energy by rotating a coil in a magnetic field.

questions.text: What is total internal reflection?
questions.expected_answer: It is complete reflection of light from a denser to a rarer medium when incidence angle exceeds the critical angle.

questions.text: State the conditions for total internal reflection.
questions.expected_answer: Light must travel from denser to rarer medium and the angle of incidence must be greater than the critical angle.

questions.text: Define critical angle.
questions.expected_answer: Critical angle is the angle of incidence in denser medium for which the angle of refraction is 90 degrees.

questions.text: What causes a mirage in deserts?
questions.expected_answer: A mirage is caused by refraction and total internal reflection in air layers of varying density due to temperature differences.

questions.text: How does optical fiber transmit light?
questions.expected_answer: Optical fiber transmits light by repeated total internal reflection in its core.

questions.text: State Huygens' principle.
questions.expected_answer: Every point on a wavefront acts as a source of secondary wavelets and the new wavefront is their forward envelope.

questions.text: What is interference of light?
questions.expected_answer: Interference is redistribution of light energy due to superposition of waves from coherent sources.

questions.text: Define coherent sources of light.
questions.expected_answer: Coherent sources emit waves of the same frequency with a constant phase difference.

questions.text: What happens to fringe width in Young's double slit experiment if the apparatus is immersed in water?
questions.expected_answer: Fringe width decreases because wavelength decreases in water.

questions.text: Distinguish between diffraction and interference.
questions.expected_answer: Interference arises from superposition from coherent sources, while diffraction arises from superposition of wavelets from parts of the same wavefront.
